coredata.dc:title: Functionalized starch for formulation of graphitic carbon nanodots as viricidal/anticancer laborers
coredata.dc:creator: Emam, Hossam E.
El-Shahat, Mahmoud
Allayeh, Abdou K.
Ahmed, Hanan B.
coredata.dc:description: Biocompatibility of carbon nanodots (CNDs) ingrained from biopolymers are considered as prerequisite characters for their successive exploitation in different biomedical purposes. CNDs are known to be categorized to carbogenic nanodots (CgNDs) and graphitic nanodots (GNDs). The point of novelty in the current approach is to study the effect of chemical medication for starch before and after its functionalization with glucose, to ingrain carbon nanodots, that were sequentially applicable as viricidal and anticancer laborers. The represented data revealed that, CgNDs were nucleated from alkali-hydrolyzed starch exhibited with particle size of 4.8 ± 1.8 nm, whereas, glucose-functionalized starch was successfully exploited for ingraining of GNDs with particle size of 3.1 ± 1.3 nm. The viricidal action of the prepared CgNDs and GNDs against Low Pathogenic Coronavirus (229E) was estimated via CPE-inhibition Assay and the obtained IC50 was 61.2 and 29.6 mg/mL for CgNDs and GNDs, respectively. The synthesized CgNDs and GNDs were tested against human non-small cell lung cancer cell line (NSCLC, A549) via Sulforhodamine B (SRB) assay and the estimated IC50 was 356.5 and 220.3 μg/mL in case of CgNDs and GNDs, respectively. The obtained data approved the seniority of GNDs over CgNDs to be applicable as antiviral and antitumor laborers.

head.ce:title: Functionalized starch for formulation of graphitic carbon nanodots as viricidal/anticancer laborers

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Biocompatibility of carbon nanodots (CNDs) ingrained from biopolymers are considered as prerequisite characters for their successive exploitation in different biomedical purposes. CNDs are known to be categorized to carbogenic nanodots (CgNDs) and graphitic nanodots (GNDs). The point of novelty in the current approach is to study the effect of chemical medication for starch before and after its functionalization with glucose, to ingrain carbon nanodots, that were sequentially applicable as viricidal and anticancer laborers. The represented data revealed that, CgNDs were nucleated from alkali-hydrolyzed starch exhibited with particle size of 4.8 ± 1.8 nm, whereas, glucose-functionalized starch was successfully exploited for ingraining of GNDs with particle size of 3.1 ± 1.3 nm. The viricidal action of the prepared CgNDs and GNDs against Low Pathogenic Coronavirus (229E) was estimated via CPE-inhibition Assay and the obtained IC50 was 61.2 and 29.6 mg/mL for CgNDs and GNDs, respectively. The synthesized CgNDs and GNDs were tested against human non-small cell lung cancer cell line (NSCLC, A549) via Sulforhodamine B (SRB) assay and the estimated IC50 was 356.5 and 220.3 μg/mL in case of CgNDs and GNDs, respectively. The obtained data approved the seniority of GNDs over CgNDs to be applicable as antiviral and antitumor laborers.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanodots (CNDs) is a unique member of carbon nanomaterials. The significances of CNDs are attributed to their superior properties such as low toxicity, preferable biocompatibility, high-water solubility, high sensitivity, selectivity to target analytic, and superior photo-stabilization (Ding et al., 2013; Krysmann et al., 2012; Song et al., 2014). A number of techniques for synthesis of CNDs were reported such as hydrothermal and solvothermal techniques and requires simple equipment. On the other hand, metal nanoclusters (NCs) and nanodots (NDs) show biocompatibility, strong photoluminescence with high stability, large stokes shifts, and high solubility (Gallo et al., 2016; Vasimalai and Fernandez-Argüelles, 2018). Owing to their superior fluorescent character, CNDs were made an impressive stride in the selectivity and sensitivity to diverse arrays of organic/biological compounds, salt ions and target gases (Lai et al., 2013; Shoujon et al., 2012; Sun et al., 2012, 2013; Wu and Chiu, 2013).

Progressing in synthesis and applications of CNDs as nano probes could be ascribed to still in its infancy, however, continuous progression might result in their integration into biological and environmental applications. CNDs basically are categorized to two major types of carbogenic nanodots (CgNDs) and graphitic NDs (GNDs). These types could be nucleated from different natural precursors, such as carbohydrates and proteins via the hydrothermal, solvothermal and microwave synthetic methodologies (Hong et al., 2009, 2011; Mirtchev et al., 2012; Zhu et al., 2013). The synthesized polymeric layered CNDs were reported to contain nitrogen, sulfur, oxygen hetero-atoms with carbon atoms in the main skeleton as the main elemental structure with sp2 hybridization and conjugative system with plenty of mobile electrons.

GNDs are reported to be mainly composed of sp 2 hybridized/conjugated carbon atoms with or without S, N, O atoms. CgNDs are conjugated systems carbons atoms with sp 2 and sp 3 hybridization and might contain oxygen-containing groups. CNDs prepared via the hydrothermal technique are also reported to be nucleated via ionization, condensation, polymerization and carbonization through bottom-up mechanism. Moreover, green synthetic routes were interestingly considered as alterative methodologies for chemical methods (Maruthupandi et al., 2022a, 2022b). It was reported that, CNDs might contain mobile electrons in their polymeric layers to limit their particle size of 2–8 nm and could exhibit a superior/non-blinking photoluminescent properties and absorption of UV–visible radiations (Gupta et al., 2011; Mirtchev et al., 2012; Shen et al., 2012a, 2012b; Zhang et al., 2013). Therefore, these CNDs could be applicable for coating the biomaterials, nano-probes, nano-vectors for bio-medical purposes (Bacon et al., 2014; Li, L. et al., 2013; Lin et al., 2014; Zhang et al., 2012; Zhou et al., 2013).

Different nanomaterials were reported for their applicability in different biological purposes (Vasimalai, 2018). However, the biocompatibility and non-toxicity properties of CNDs are considered as prerequisite characters for their applicability in different biomedical purposes (Dias et al., 2019; Vasimalai et al., 2018), to reflect its importance to be uniquely discovered for exploitation in cancer treatment and viral infections. Some of researches considerably interested in the biotoxicity of CNDs against various cancer cell lines such as Hep-2 (Wu et al., 2014), HeLa (Fan et al., 2014) and A549 (Sachdev and Gopinath, 2015; Wu, Z.L. et al., 2013). Moreover, it was confirmed that, some of CNDs clustered from green carbon precursors showed excellent fluorescent activities and biological properties such as antioxidizing, antitumor and anti-inflammatory impacts (Hsu et al., 2013; Li et al., 2018; Vasimalai et al., 2018; Xu et al., 2016; Zhao et al., 2015). However, few reports were considered with investigating the antiviral potency of CNDs. While, Dong et al. studied the effects of direct treatment with 5 μg/mL of EDA-CQDs or EPA-CQD against human noroviruses (virus-like-particles; VLPs), GI.1 and GII.4 VLPs, and they reported that, CNDs were formidably characterized with effective antiviral performance via prevent of VLPs bonding to the histo-blood group antigen (HBGA) receptors on human cells (Dong et al., 2017). Recently, Huang, et al. were affirmed that, CNDs nucleated from benzoxazine monomers could in-vitro prevent the infection with life-threatening porcine parvovirus, flaviviruses and adenovirus-associated virus, as it was suggested to be through the direct interaction with the surface of the virion and starts contacting with the viral cell (Huang et al., 2019).

The current report focuses on the exploration of a synthetic route that is more economical via the utilization of greener chemistry aspects with diversification of possible precursors. Starch was selected as a precursor for CNDs attributing to its biocompatibility nature, to give the ingrained CNDs a better chance for the applicability with low toxicity tolerance. The current study is designed for monitoring the effect of chemical modification for starch in clustering of GNDs with high fluorescence and excellent bio-active properties. Chemical modification of starch was proceeded via functionalization of starch with glucose to produce glucose-functionalized starch (TM). Alkali hydrolyzed glucose, starch and glucose-functionalized starch were examined for their compatibility in nucleation of either CgNDs or GNDs under hydrothermal conditions. In the currently presented research, glucose was selected for functionalization of starch in order to act as the carbon source attributing to its well-known low temperature of carbonization, whereas starch could act as the capping and since the as-prepared CNDs were supposed to be have functionalization and passivation surface states with carbonyl or hydroxyl groups.

In accordance to our knowledge, no researches were studied the effect of polysaccharide functionalization for ingraining of GNDs with the anticancer and antiviral performance. The current study focused on the effect of chemical medication of starch for ingraining of GNDs under hydrothermal conditions in order to be applicable concurrently as viricidal and anticancer laborer. The current study represented the efficiency of glucose, starch versus glucose-functionalized starch as bioactive reagents, to highlight the effect of functionalization for starch on the bio-activity of the nucleated CNDs. Successive nucleation of carbon nanoparticles (CNPs), CgNDs and GNDs by exploitation of glucose, starch and glucose-functionalized starch, respectively, was affirmed via several instrumental characterizations, like, UV–Visible spectroscopy, Transmission Electron Microscopy, FTIR, 1HNMR and 13CNMR. Subsequently, the photoluminescent properties of the prepared CNDs were monitored. The antiviral and anticancer performance of the prepared CNPs, CNDs and GNDs was tested against Low Pathogenic Coronavirus (229E) and lung cancer cells (A549), respectively.

d -Glucose, Starch (from Maize), methanol; acetic acid, 2-propanol and DMF were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Company. All materials were used without further purification. De-ionized water was used in all experimental processes.

Starch (250 mg, 1.54 mmol) was dissolved in 6 mL NaOH. After complete dissolution, the solution was heated at 90 °C and a solution of d-Glucose (277 mg, 1.54 mmol) in 10 mL water was added drop wisely under air flow. After 6 h, the homogenous hot mixture was precipitated into 200 mL 2-propanol drop wisely. The product was filtered and washed with 2-propanol to remove all un-reacted d-Glucose and dried under vacuum at 85 °C for 24 h.

For hydrolysis of Glucose-Functionalized Starch (TM), 10 g/L of NaOH was added to 10 g/L of TM to be subsequently left under magnetic stirring for 30 min at 100 °C (Table 1 ), in order to hydrolyze and depolymerize starch under alkaline condition as a first supposed step of NDs nucleation. Afterwards, under hydrothermal conditions for complete clustering of CNDs, in vertical hydrothermal autoclave reactor, the reaction colloid was transferred and left in oven for 6 h. The hydrothermal reaction was currently proceeded at low temperature (180 °C), as the reaction temperature must be not quite high (>300 °C) to ensure the complete carbonization of molecular precursor with avoiding the deterioration of the capping reagent (starch macromolecules) as it might lead to bad surface passivation, and losing (or denaturation) of the functional groups. Lastly, the reaction colloid with dark brown color was cooled at room temperature to be dialyzed with distilled water for ultra-purification to produce ultrafiltered/mono-dispersed/purified CNDs for further characterization.

Investigation of the chemical composition of the condensation products (target molecule, TM) from the interaction of glucose with the exploited polysaccharide (starch) and CNDs that ingrained from such prepared TM were affirmed from the different spectral mapping data. Infrared spectral analysis was performed by using Jasco FT/IR 6100 spectrometer. Absorbance spectral data were estimated at 500–4000 cm−1 using 15 points smoothing, 4 cm−1 resolution, 64 scanning times with scanning rate of 2 mm s−1. Additionally, the spectra of nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR and 13C NMR) were recorded on a Jeol-Ex-300 NMR spectrometer (JEOL – Japan). Photoluminescence of CNDs in ultraviolet–visible range were analysed via Spectro-fluorometer (JASCO FP8300). Spectral analyses were carried out at room temperature with excitation at 340 nm, while, emission was detected at room temperature. Topographical features and size distribution of the ingrained CNDs were estimated via anticipation of High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope from Japan (JEOL-JEM-1200). Size distribution of CNDs was evaluated by 4 pi analysis software (from USA) for at least fifty particles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sulforhodamine B (SRB) assay and anticancer potency
ce:section.ce:para: Sulforhodamine B (SRB) assay is considered as one of the most widely applicable methodology for in vitro cytotoxicity screening (Skehan et al., 1990). The assay depends on the affinity of SRB for chemical bonding to the protein components of the tested cancer cells (human non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) cell line, A549) that were fixed to the tissue culture plates by trichloroacetic acid (TCA). SRB is bright-pink amino-xanthene dye with two sulfonic groups that chemically bonded to the basic amino-acid moieties under a mild acidic condition, and decompose under a basic condition (Lillie, 1977). As the chemical bonding of SRB is stoichiometric, the content of the dye extracted from the stained cells is directly proportional to the cell mass. The sulforhodamine B (SRB) assay is applied for the cell density detection, based on the estimation of the cellular protein content. The methodology ascribed to be optimized for the toxicity screening of components to adherent cells in a 96-well format. After incubation, the cell monolayers are fixed with 10% (wt/vol) trichloroacetic acid to be stained for 30 min, afterwards the excess dye is eliminated with repeated washing cycles using 1% (vol/vol) acetic acid. Protein-bonded dye is dissolved in 10 mM Tris base solution at 510 nm via a microplate reader. The data are linear over a 20-fold range of cell numbers (AbouAitah et al., 2021).

The CPE-inhibition assay will be used to identify potential antivirals against Low Pathogenic Coronavirus (229E). The dose-response assay was designed to determine the range of efficacy for the chosen antiviral, i.e., the 50% inhibitory concentration (IC50), as well as the range of cytotoxicity (CC50). This assay is critical for determining antiviral efficacy in cell culture systems. Nawah-Scientific, Egypt, provided the Low Pathogenic Coronavirus (229E) and Vero E6 cells. Vero E6 cells were grown in DMEM medium supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum and 0.1% antibiotic/antimycotic solution. Gibco BRL provided the antibiotic & antimycotic solution, trypsin-EDTA, fetal bovine serum, and DMEM medium (Grand Island, NY, USA). The Crystal violet method was used to evaluate antiviral activity and cytotoxicity assays using the recently reported for the cytopathic (CPE) inhibition effect. In brief, Vero E6 cells were seeded into a 96-well culture plate at a density of 2 × 104 cells/well one day before infection. The culture medium was removed the next day, and the cells were washed with phosphate-buffered saline.

The infectivity of Low Pathogenic Coronavirus (229E) was determined using the crystal violet method (AbouAitah et al., 2021), which monitored CPE and allowed the percentage of cell viability to be calculated. 0.1 mL of diluted virus suspension of 229E containing CCID50 (1.0 × 106) of virus stock was added to mammalian cells. This dose was selected to produce the desired CPEs three days after infection. For compound treatments, 0.01 mL of medium containing the desired compound concentration was added to the cells. Each test sample's antiviral activity was determined using a 10-fold diluted concentration range of 0.1–1000 μg/ml. The virus controls (virus-infected, nondrug-treated cells) and cell controls (non-infected, nondrug treated cells) were tested. For 72 h, culture plates were incubated at 37 °C in 5% CO2. The development of cytopathic effect was monitored by light microscopy. Following a PBS wash, the cell monolayers were fixed and stained with a 0.03% crystal violet solution in 2% ethanol and 10% formalin. After washing and drying the optical density of individual wells was quantified spectrophotometrically at 570/630 nm.

The percentage of antiviral activities of the test's compounds were calculated according to Pauwels et al. (1988). Based on these results, the 50% CPE inhibitory dose (IC50) was calculated. Before this assay, we assessed the cytotoxicity; cells were seeded at a density of 2 × 104 cells/well in a 96-well culture plate. The next day, the culture medium containing serially diluted samples was added to the cells and incubated for 72 h before being removed and the cells washed with PBS. The following steps were carried out in the same manner as described above for the antiviral activity assay. The results of the 50% cytotoxic concentrations (CC50) and the 50% inhibitory concentration (IC50) were determined using GraphPad PRISM software (Graph-Pad Software, San Diego, USA).

The current approach is considered with investigation of green synthetic route with diversifying possible precursors that were selected due to its biocompatibility, which will subsequently stand a better chance for the application that requires low toxicity tolerance. Starch as one of highly abundant polysaccharides is found to be widely applicable in different fields. This could imply the essentiality for different unique characters with respect to those of the pristine starch, basically achievable via the chemical modification. In the recent decades, different products with unique or enhanced characters were produced from starch, attributing to the adoption of “green” solvents i.e., ionic liquids and supercritical CO2and different new methodologies (regioselective derivatizing, atom transfer radical polymerizing, etc.) that are ascribed with as controlled modification. Conventional chemical modification methods (cationization, oxidation, esterification, etherification, and crosslinking), are often performed via the reaction of starch with different small molecules. Starch derivatives that were modified via the substitution reaction of OH groups with small molecules were reported to exhibit different unique characters that could expand the applicability of starch-based reagents as high value products (Fan and Picchioni, 2020).

Herein, a comparative study between the affinities of glucose versus starch and glucose-functionalized starch for controllable ingraining of either CgNDs or GNDs, was performed under basic conditions via hydrothermal technique. As schematically represented in Fig. 1 ; under the effect of strong alkali, starch macromolecules supposed to undergo condensation reaction with glucose moieties. The water molecule was released and the oxygen bridge was formed to produce the as-required glucose-functionalized starch (Amos et al., 2021; Egharevba, 2019).

The mechanism of CNDs nucleation from glucose-functionalized starch could be postulated as follows; successive ingraining of CNDs is proceeded via two main steps; carbonization and surface passivation (surface functionalization). In carbonization glucose as the organic precursor is converted into its basic carbon containing residues that was currently nucleated under hydrothermal conditions. Passivation as the next step is required in order to minimize the effect of the surface-defect, trap sites, and the direct quenching from the surrounding medium, for enhancement of the photoemission. Whereas, the pure nucleated carbon nano-objects usually were not exhibited with optical properties. Meanwhile, the surface treatment for functionalization with starch as covering materials or capping agents is important step for controllable in growth of CNDs to introduce an insulative shell with decorative functional groups on carbon nanoparticles surface.

Starch was hypothesized to be functionalized as capping agents for well surface passivation for decoration of the ingrained CNDs with reducible/functional groups (carbonyl& hydroxyl groups). In accordance to literature (da Silva and Gonçalves, 2011; Kwon et al., 2013; Li, Y. et al., 2013; Liang et al., 2013; Liu et al., 2011; Wu, L. et al., 2013), it could be hypothesized that, glucose as molecular precursor was exploited for ingraining of CNDs capped with starch under basic medium via hydrothermal technique, as it advantageously exhibited with low carbonization temperature (180 °C). Moreover, dialysis was performed lastly for ultrafiltration to obtain highly purified/mono-dispersed CNDs with controllable topographical features (Li et al., 2011). According to literature (Chandra et al., 2012; Dong et al., 2012; Kim et al., 2013), excellent optical characters of CNDs could be arisen via surface passivation with oxygen containing groups of the covering materials or capping agents.
FT-IR, 1HNMR & 13CNMR

Fig. 2 displayed FT-IR spectral mapping data of native glucose, starch and glucose-functionalized starch (TM). Whereas, for native glucose, the absorption bands at 3311 cm−1, 3007 cm−1, 29290 cm−1, 1439 cm−1 and 1024 cm−1 that are characteristic for O–H stretching, asymmetric vibration of C–H, symmetric vibration of C–H, bending vibration of C–H, vibration of C–O and C–C, respectively (Shaw and Mantsch, 2006). Whereas, FTIR spectrum of starch revealed that, the broad band at 3335 cm−1 is characteristic for the stretching mode of O–H groups, while, the adsorption band at 1665 cm−1 is assigned to an intermolecular H-bond, and the peaks at 1158 cm−1, 2943 cm−1 are corresponding to C–O and C–H stretching, respectively (Hebeish et al., 2009). After functionalization of starch with glucose, all the specific bands of glucose and starch were retained, however, a weak band at 1698 cm−1 assigned to C=O stretching. A characteristic band for glycosidic linkage is obviously detected at 1084-1156 cm−1 in the spectra of starch and TM.

On the other hand, NMR spectral mapping data were presented in Fig. 3 for TM. From Fig. 3a that presented 1HNMR for TM, it could be estimated that, 1H NMR (400 MHz, DMSO): δ = 3.62–3.49 (m, 3H, 2 starch-H + OH), 3.89 (s, 2H, COCH2), 4.03–3.98 (m, 2H, glucose-H + OH), 4.35–4.23 (m, 2H, CH2OH), 4.39 (dd, J = 16.0, 2.4 Hz, 2H, OCH2), 4.79–4.85 (m, 4H, glucose-2H+2OH), 4.92–4.87 (m, 4H, starch-2H+2OH), 5.23 (t, J = 8.7 Hz, 1H, starch-H). moreover, the presence of singlet signal at 3.89 ppm in H NMR spectrum corresponding to new formed CH2 group besides presences the all the characteristics signals attributed for both glucose and starch moieties. Fig. 3b showed 13CNMR for TM, whereas, 13C NMR (100 MHz, DMSO): δ = 44.44, 45.55, 59.52, 66.98, 72.27, 78.22, 82.00, 83.49, 83.84, 85.14, 105.46, 182.26. Additionally, the presence of signal at 78.22 and 182.26 ppm in 13CNMR spectrum corresponding to new formed CH2 and C=O groups besides the presence of all the characteristic signals attributed for both glucose and starch moieties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Size distribution for the ingrained CNDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 represented TEM micrographs for CNDs ingrained from glucose, starch and TM after dialysis to show the topographical features of the nucleated CNDs, whereas their size distributions were also estimated. From the plotted micrographs, the compatibility of starch its vital role in well surface passivation of CNDs for ingraining of spherical/highly monodisperse CNDs, were affirmed. From the estimated data, it could be depicted that; the nanostructures that were exhibited with particle size more than 10 nm ascribed as carbon nanoparticles (CNPs), nucleated from alkali hydrolyzed glucose, i.e., glucose was successfully exploited in production of (CNPs) carbon nanoparticles under basic condition with size distribution of 15.5 ± 6.8 nm. Alkaline hydrolysis of starch macromolecules is resulted in ingraining of CNDs with size distribution of 4.8 ± 1.8 nm. These could be attributed to the effect of pH in enhancement of the polymer accessibility to produce alkali fragmented moieties that were superiorly exploited for generation of more controllable sized CNDs, as reported in literature (Ahmed et al., 2021; Ahmed and Emam, 2020; Emam et al., 2021). Well surface passivation via exploitation of starch as covering material to form an insulative shell with decorative functional groups is approved from the estimated data, while, the particle size was significantly diminished by functionalization of the TM as molecular precursors of CNDs. Whereas, under alkaline conditions, exploitation of TM as molecular precursor produced CNDs with the smallest particle size of 3.1 ± 1.3 nm.

In contrast to any other category of nanomaterials, the optical properties of CNDs were mainly dependent on well surface passivation to be covered with insulative shells with decorative functional groups (Gao et al., 2016). Wu et al. affirmed that, the optical properties of CNDs is basically correlated to the surface states and its chemical composition with decorative functional groups on graphite [34]. The surface states are optically active centers composed via the synergetic hybridization of the functional groups and carbonic cores (Zhang et al., 2010). Therefore, the photoluminescence of the ingrained CNDs is owing to the decorative groups ingrained from starch or chitosan with overlapping of n-π* and π-π* electronic transitions for the aromatized nucleated CNDs. Moreover, the surface passivation mainly effects on the absorption locations, resulting in the observable coloration of the colloidal solution of CNDs and endowing their accessible colorimetric detection.

Herein, the photoluminescence of the ingrained CNDs was estimated via PL spectroscopy and the PL emission spectral data after excitation at 340 nm were presented in Fig. 5 . Fig. 5a showed images of the colloidal solutions for S1, S2 & S3 under UV-lamp (365 nm), to obviously observe the interchanging of color from creamy white (S1), to pale yellow (S2) and dark yellow (S3) giving a sign by exploiting glucose, starch and TM as molecular precursors for successive ingraining of carbon nanoparticles and nanodots. Fig. 6 b represented the excitation spectra for S3 (at 460 nm) and showed that there were two excitation peaks at 260 nm (low intense) and 360 nm (high intense). Hence, the emission spectra were checked at two excitation wavelength 300 and 360 nm. Fig. 5c and d and are plotted for the emission spectra of S1, S2 & S3 nano-colloids with excitation at 300 nm & 360 nm, respectively. From the presented spectra, it could be declared that, excitation at 360 nm resulted in detection of emission spectra with the highest fluorescence intensity. From Fig. 5d, the excited CNDs derivatized from TM were exhibited with florescent intense band at 445–480 nm after dialysis that is corresponding to the green region (Ahmed and Emam, 2020; Emam and Ahmed, 2021; Li et al., 2017; Sasikala et al., 2016; Yew et al., 2017). The estimated broad intense peaks in ultraviolet–visible absorption spectrum affirmed the inter-composition of mobile pi-electrons of conjugated carbonic building blocks and non-bonding of hetero atoms for CNDs ingrained from both starch and TM. Exhibition of strong fluorescence in visible range will consequently facilitate their application in biomedical fields and has currently elaborated by the photoluminescence spectrum of CNDs.

Additionally, from the estimated data, it could be depicted that, the fluorescence intensity was extensively increased with the existence of polysaccharide in the nano-colloid to affirm the vital role of polysaccharide as capping agents for well surface passivation of CNDs. While, the most intense fluorescence peaks were estimated when CNDs ingrained under basic conditions from glucose that was passivated with starch as organic moieties with oxygen containing decorative groups as outstanding dopant in accordance to literature (Wu et al., 2017), to demonstrate that, PL centers of CNDs might be originated from the surface energy traps to become emissive upon stabilizing as a result of surface passivation. In summarization, it could be decided that, CNDs with long-wavelength and blue emission could be harvested by means of selecting glucose with surface passivation by exploiting starch biopolymer.
FT-IR, 1HNMR &13CNMR spectra for CNDs.

Fig. 6 represents FT-IR spectral results of S1, S2 & S3 after their exploitation in ingraining of nanostructures under hydrothermal conditions. The data showed that, under hydrothermal conditions the characteristic peaks of glucose and starch were retained, whereas, the characteristic peak of CNDs ingrained from starch & TM was observably estimated at 1410-1450 cm−1 that is characteristic for C–C aromatic ring chain vibrations. Additionally, the peak of glycosidic linkage (1084-1156 cm−1) was significantly decreased, while the carbonyl peak (1665/1698 cm−1) was disappeared.

On the other hand, in Figs. 7 and 8
NMR spectral results were plotted for further approval of the mechanism of CNDs ingraining. Fig. 7 represented 1HNMR for S1, S2 & S3 that were prepared under the hydrothermal conditions. While, Fig. 7a and b represented that the spectral data of S1& S2, the protons attached to carbonyl groups were estimated at 4.8–5.0 ppm, whereas, the spectra for CNDs ingrained from S3 showed that, beside the as-mentioned band for protons attached to the carbonyl groups, a weak characteristic band for protons of sp2 CH=CH at 8.2 ppm is also observed.

Fig. 8a represented the spectrum for S1, where, the characteristic peaks of sp3 carbons (20 ppm) and carbons attached with hydroxyl groups (68 ppm) were shown. Whereas, Fig. 8b showed the characteristic band at 20 ppm corresponding to sp3 carbons, the typical band for sp3 carbons at 44.5 ppm and a characteristic band corresponding to C=C aromatic or sp2 carbons at 164 ppm were observed for S2. Fig. 8c represented 13CNMR spectra for CNDs ingrained from TM and it could be obviously shown that, typical bands at 20 ppm, 68 ppm and weak band at 183 ppm, which are corresponding to sp3 carbons, carbons attached with hydroxyl groups, and C=C aromatic or sp2 carbons, respectively. So, in accordance to literature (Gupta et al., 2011; Hong et al., 2009, 2011; Mirtchev et al., 2012; Shen et al., 2012a, 2012b; Zhu et al., 2013), it could be affirmed that, under hydrothermal conditions, alkali hydrolyzed glucose generated CNPs, whereas, CgNDs was nucleated from alkali hydrolyzed starch, while, GNDs were successively clustered from glucose-functionalized starch.

World widely, different diseases that resulted from viral infection could dramatically cause eventual death. Treatment for most of these diseases became interestingly considered researching points especially if the pathogens were modified to become resistant for different drugs, unfortunately results in higher medical costing and rising of death rate. Few studies were recently considered with investigation of the potentiality of carbon-based nanostructures for viricidal action and/or decreasing the infection rate. Du et al., were reported that carbon-based nanostructures could superiorly exhibit viricidal action for pseudo rabies virus as example for DNA virus and porcine reproductive respiratory syndrome virus as a type of RNA virus via stopping their reproduction (Du et al., 2016). Type I interferons (IFNs) as two main categories of IFN-α and IFN-β, are ascribed as the best instinctive viricidal immune reagents with a excellent viricidal affinity to the human viral infection (Gomez et al., 2005). Therefore, it was assumed that, the viricidal effects of carbon-based nanostructures was attributed to the motivation for the generation of ISGs (Interferon Simulated Genes) and activation for IFN-α, that subsequently results in stopping the viral multiplication (Du et al., 2016). Another approach reported that, carbon based nanostructures could act as antiviral reagent via motivation of apoptosis, that was mediated principally via generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), motivation releasing of cytochrome c from mitochondria, activation of caspase3, cleavage of phospholipase C-γ1, and eventually degrading DNA moieties (Woo et al., 2003).

Currently, a comparative study between the viricidal performances of the as-prepared nano-colloids (S1, S2 & S3) against Low Pathogenic Coronavirus (229E) was studied. In the current report, The CPE-inhibition assay will be used to identify potential antivirals against Low Pathogenic Coronavirus (229E). The antiviral action of the prepared nano-colloids is estimated via the optical activity. Additionally, the dose-response assay was proceeded in order to estimate the range of affinity for the prepared nano-colloids, i.e., the 50% inhibitory concentration (IC50), as well as the range of cytotoxicity (CC50). The infectivity of Low Pathogenic Coronavirus (229E) was detected via the crystal violet methodology, which monitored CPE to calculate the cell viability percent. Fig. 9 showed the relation between the concentration of the prepared nano-colloids and the optical density & cell viability percent. From the plotted figures, it could be clarified that, the optical density was shown to be gradually decreased with increment the dose/concentration of the prepared nano-colloids. For more affirmation, the cell viability percent is shown to be gradually decreased with increment log value of the dose (μg/mL).

From Fig. 9 and b for S1 & S2 (CNPs), optical density was decreased from 2.53 & 2.81 to 1.44 & 1.85 with increment of the dose form 125 × 103 μg/mL to 1000 × 103 μg/mL, respectively. Whereas, the cell viability percent was estimated to be 65.8% and 52.1% for 250 × 103 μg/mL for S1 & S2, respectively. Fig. 9c is shown for GNDs (S3), optical density was diminished from 3.05 to 1.53 with rising of the concentration form 125 × 103 μg/mL to 1000 × 103 μg/mL and the cell viability percent was estimated to be 33.5% for 250 × 103 μg/mL, Fig. 9d showed the relation between the concentration of the applied samples (S1, S2 & S3) as antiviral reagents and the estimated values of CC50 & IC50. From the represented data, it could be declared that, CC50 was estimated for S1, S2 & S3 at concentration of 170.1, 99.0 & 72.4 mg/mL, respectively. The IC50 for the exploited samples was evaluated for S1, S2 & S3 at concentrations of 76.7, 29.6 & 61.2 mg/mL, respectively.

SRB assay is reported to be ascribed to be applicable for large-scaled screening, as well as in researching, whereas, this assay is widely applied for drug-toxicity examination against different non-cancerous and cancerous cells (Monks et al., 1991). sulforhodamine B (SRB) assay is applied for determination of cell density, based on estimation of the content cellular proteins.SRB is a dye of a bright-pinkamino-xanthene with two sulfonic groups that under mild acidic conditions could be bonded to basic amino-acid moieties, and dissociate under alkaline conditions (Lillie, 1977). Whereas, binding of SRB is stoichiometric, the dye extracted from stained cells contents is directly proportional to the cell mass.

The mortal action of any reagent against a live cell is known as the cytotoxic effect, whereas, such reagent was supposed to act in cell death via the motivation of the necrosis that means accidental cell death, or apoptosis, i.e., programmed cell death. In agreement with literature (Arkan et al., 2018), apoptosis that is described as programmed cell-death is known as a preferable mechanism of action for the anticancer reagents. Recent reports were interestingly considered with investigating of potential laborers for inducing the apoptosis in the malignant cell. Activating of caspases is reported to act as a principal step for motivating the apoptosis. Caspase-3 is one of endoproteases i.e., an enzyme that could control the inflammation and apoptosis signal networks. Caspase-3 is also an executioner caspase that has a main role in coordinating the cellular deterioration, i.e., DNA fragmentation or degradation of cytoskeletal proteins (Elmore, 2007).

Fig. 10 showed that, the optical density was greatly lowered via exposure to nano-colloids of S1, S2 & S3, while, higher concentrations were observably leaded to vanish the optical density, however, gradual decrement in the optical density was observably significant in case of GNDs (S3) compared to that prepared from S1 & S2. From Fig. 10a and b for S1 & S2, the optical density was diminished from 2.88 & 2.25 to 1.60 & 0.93 with increment of the dose form 125–1000 μg/mL, respectively. The cell viability percentage was calculated to be 40.7% & 27.4 for 1000 μg/mL from S1 & S2, respectively, whereas, by increasing concentration of the dose, the cell viability was obviously decreased. Fig. 10c is presented for GNDs (S3), the optical density was decreased from 2.07 to 0.80 with increment the concentration form 125–1000 μg/mL and the cell viability percentage was evaluated to be 23.0% only for 1000 μg/mL. The higher cytotoxic impacts for the prepared samples is resulted in decreasing the number of viable cells, that in turn reflected in lower amounts of SRB dye and subsequently, decreasing the optical density. Therefore, following up the decrement of the optical density was important for monitoring the decrement in the amounts of the viable cells. Fig. 10d showed the relationship between the concentration of the applied samples (S1, S2 & S3) as anticancer reagents and the evaluated values of IC50. From the plotted results, it could be depicted that, IC50 for the applied samples was detected for S1, S2 & S3 at concentrations of 475.9, 356.5 & 220.3 μg/mL, respectively.

In agreement to the currently investigated anticancer potency of the prepared samples, it could be approved that, 300 μg/mL of GNDs colloid ingrained from the currently prepared TM could be promisingly applicable as a new platform for anticancer laborers with mortality percent higher than 50%. These findings significantly confirmed that; the nano-colloids that were currently prepared in the presented approach were potentially exhibited significant mortal effects against human non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) cell line, A549, however, GNDs nucleated from glucose-functionalized starch (TM) showed more affinity for cytotoxic action and anti-cancer performance against the as-tested lung cancer cell line rather than that synthesized from glucose or starch macromolecules. According to literature (Milner et al., 2002), the anticancer performance and anti-proliferative action of the prepared GNDs in the current work could illustrated as, exposing to GNDs was mainly resulted in activating of caspase-3 of lung cancer cell lines, and consequently leading to apoptotic cell death, as the nucleated CNDs with structure containing free mobile electrons and it could inhibit the growth of the as-examined lung cancer cells by generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) for activation of caspase-3, that could act directly in apoptosis. It could be concluded that, the synthesized GNDs ingrained from glucose-functionalized starch in the current approach could be described as superiorly bio-applicable anticancer candidates. Moreover, the data approved the seniority of GNDs to be applicable as antitumor laborers over CgNDs and CNPs.

The effect of chemical medication of starch for ingraining of GNDs under hydrothermal conditions was presented. The current study focused on the efficiency of glucose, starch versus glucose-functionalized starch on the bio-activity of the nucleated CNDs. Successive nucleation of CNPs and either CgNDs or GNDs by exploitation of glucose, starch and glucose-functionalized starch, respectively, was affirmed via several instrumental characterizations including TEM, FTIR, 1HNMR and 13CNMR. Subsequently, the photoluminescent properties of the prepared CNPs &CNDs were monitored. The antiviral and anticancer performance of the obtained CNPs, CgNDs and GNDs were tested lung cancer cell (A549) and Low Pathogenic Coronavirus (229E), respectively. The results showed that, the GNDs ingrained (3.1 ± 1.3 nm) from glucose-functionalized starch showed superiorly antiviral and anticancer candidates. The IC50 values for obtained GNDs against lung cancer cell (A549) and Coronavirus (229E) were 6.2 mg/mL and 220.3 μg/mL, respectively. The Therefore, the currently prepared GNDs could be promisingly applicable as a new platform for antiviral and anticancer laborers. Additionally, the current findings will open the way for studying the modification of biopolymers in order to obtain oriented highly reactive CNDs.
The authors declare that they have no conflict of interest.
